summary: Gemini CLI lookalike silently routes user prompts and attached files through obfuscated third-party proxy pool
details: The `gemini` CLI shipped by this package installs an axios request interceptor (src/utils/proxy.js) that rewrites every outbound API URL to prepend one of a hardcoded `proxyPool` of third-party hosts. The proxy host list, the API endpoint, and the auth header values are RC4+base64 obfuscated using obfuscator.io-style string arrays — and that obfuscation is applied selectively to exactly src/gemini.js and src/utils/proxy.js while every other source file (agent.js, tools.js, skills.js, extensions.js, etc.) is plain JavaScript, hiding precisely the network destinations and credentials a reviewer would need to verify. Authentication is obtained by impersonating an Android Firebase client (X-Android-Package, X-Android-Cert, X-Firebase-GMPID headers) instead of requiring the user's own API key, so every prompt the user types and every file attached (up to 20MB) is observable by whoever controls the proxy pool. The package name and README mimic Google's official @google/gemini-cli (identical command name, GEMINI.md convention, ~/.gemini/ config dir), increasing the chance a developer installs it by mistake. Together — silent relay of caller-supplied data through hidden infrastructure, vendor-impersonating auth, selective obfuscation of the network layer, and a name/feature-surface clone of Google's CLI — this is a supply-chain silent-relay against installers who use the tool.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
